1.synthesis:
Moses descends from Sinai for the second time with renewed covenant tablets, and the first thing he does is gather the whole congregation and repeat the Sabbath command (Exod 35:1). Before a single plank is cut or a single thread dyed for the tabernacle, the people must hear again: six days for work, the seventh day holy as a Sabbath of solemn rest to the Lord — "whoever does any work on it shall be put to death" (Exod 35:2). Chapter 31 had concluded the building instructions with the same command; now chapter 35 opens the building execution with it. The Sabbath is the bookend of the entire tabernacle project.

The specific addition here is verse 3: "You shall kindle no fire in all your dwelling places on the Sabbath day." This detail was not explicitly mentioned in the earlier Sabbath commands and may have been added to address the particular situation — skilled craftsmen working with metals and fabrics might be tempted to maintain fires through the Sabbath for the sake of the holy work. The prohibition is absolute: even for the Lord's tabernacle, no fire burns on the Lord's day.

Gill notes that Moses gathered the congregation immediately on his return from Sinai — "according to Jarchi, on the morrow after the day of atonement" — eager to begin the building project that the golden calf episode had delayed. But before the project could begin, the Sabbath principle had to be re-established. Matthew Henry observes: "The first and most necessary business of those that have received divine instructions is to communicate them to others, especially those in their charge." The people must hear the word before they can do the work.

Three verses on Sabbath regulation before sixteen chapters of building instructions: the proportion is intentional. God's rest-structure frames human creative work. Labor for God's glory does not exempt any worker from God's rhythm. The craftsman who believes his sacred task justifies Sabbath-breaking has confused devotion to God's purposes with submission to God's character. The Sabbath declares that what God builds cannot be built by those who have ceased to rest in him.
1.historical_context: The prohibition of kindling fire on the Sabbath (v 3) is the only verse in the Sabbath legislation that prohibits a specific act, rather than "work" in general. It likely addressed the metallurgical and textile work that the tabernacle construction required — smelting, forging, and dyeing all needed sustained fire. The rabbis later debated extensively which categories of labor the Sabbath prohibited, but the fire prohibition here was the most concrete early specification. Numbers 15:32-36 records a man executed for gathering sticks (presumably for fire) on the Sabbath, suggesting that this prohibition was strictly enforced in the wilderness period.
1.literary_structure: The three-verse Sabbath unit (vv 1-3) functions as the prolog to the building narrative of chs 35-40, just as the Sabbath command in 31:12-17 formed the epilog to the building instructions of chs 25-31. Together they create a structural chiasm: instruction-Sabbath // Sabbath-execution. The Sabbath command thus frames the entire tabernacle project, declaring that the divine dwelling-place is built within, not despite, the divine rest-rhythm.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry's comment that Moses published the Sabbath command first — before any building began — establishes the priority of divine ordinance over divine project. The believer who allows Christian service to consume the Sabbath (broadly understood as time set apart for rest and worship) has inverted the right order. The tabernacle was God's idea and God's priority — yet even for its building, the workers must rest. Work for God is not exempt from the principle that sustains the worker: "The sabbath was made for man" (Mark 2:27). Faithful service is impossible without renewal in God's presence.
1.christology: The fire prohibition on the Sabbath points forward to the one who himself became fire — the Spirit poured out at Pentecost as "tongues of fire" (Acts 2:3). That Pentecost fire fell on the day after the Sabbath, on the first day of the week: the rest of the old covenant completed, the new creation's fire descending. The Sabbath structure itself finds its fulfillment in Christ, who declared himself "Lord of the Sabbath" (Matt 12:8) and who himself rested in the tomb on the Sabbath before rising on the first day — the great Sabbath rest completed, the new week of creation inaugurated.

4.synthesis:
The building command is now public: Moses calls the congregation to bring offerings for the tabernacle — "whoever is of a generous heart, let him bring the Lord's contribution" (Exod 35:5). The materials listed are those from chapter 25, the original call for freewill offerings. But the description of the response transforms a legal catalogue into a narrative of grace. Both men and women came, "all whose heart stirred them" and "all whose spirit moved them" (Exod 35:21), bringing their gold jewelry, blue and purple and crimson fabrics, fine linen, goat hair, ram skins, sea-cow hides, acacia wood, oil, spices, and precious stones.

Matthew Henry observes that the offerings came from three categories of people: the wealthy (gold and silver), the skilled women ("all women whose heart stirred them up in wisdom" spun the fine thread, v 25-26), and the leaders (precious stones and spices, v 27-28). All contributed according to their means and abilities; none were excused, none were compelled. The tabernacle was not built by royal taxation but by congregational devotion.

What makes this passage remarkable in its literary and theological context is its position immediately after the golden calf disaster. The same people who gave their gold earrings for a calf now give their gold bracelets and rings for the tabernacle. The same generous impulse that was misdirected to idolatry is now redirected to the true worship of God. Grace does not produce paralysis in those who have sinned; it produces re-orientation. The same hands that stripped their ornaments for judgment now bring those ornaments — purified by repentance — as a holy offering.

The skills involved were described as Spirit-given: the women's spinning was "wisdom" (Exod 35:25), not merely skill. When ordinary abilities are offered to God and directed by the Spirit, they become sacred art. Matthew Henry notes: "Those that had skill must work; those that had wealth must give. Thus every one, according to the abilities God has given them, must do their part in supporting the interests of religion."
4.historical_context: The offering campaign described here was extraordinarily successful — so much so that the craftsmen had to tell Moses the people were bringing more than enough (Exod 36:5-7). This overabundance is a remarkable reversal of the typical ancient world's problem of insufficient resources for sacred construction. Egypt's temples were funded by state revenues; Israel's was funded by freewill overflow. The women who spun goat-hair (v 26) and fine linen (v 25) contributed skills that took significant time and labor; their work was as essential to the tabernacle as the gold donated by the leaders. The social breadth of the offering — from skilled artisan to leader to ordinary Israelite — reflects the covenant community's shared investment in the dwelling of their God.
4.literary_structure: The offering narrative (vv 4-29) divides between Moses's exhortation (vv 4-19), listing what is needed, and the people's response (vv 20-29). The response section is narrated in three waves: the whole congregation who brought materials (vv 20-22), the women who spun and wove (vv 25-26), and the leaders who brought precious stones (vv 27-28). The closing summary (v 29) ties the theme together: "all the men and women, the people of Israel, whose heart moved them to bring anything for the work that the Lord had commanded by Moses to do, brought it as a freewill offering to the Lord."
4.theological_application: The willing-heart principle runs through the entire passage: heart-stirred (v 21), heart-moved (v 22), heart-willing (v 29). The new covenant equivalent is Paul's principle that "God loves a cheerful giver" (2 Cor 9:7) — the giving that pleases God flows from transformation, not compulsion. The women's spinning wisdom anticipates the Proverbs 31 woman whose hands are busy with distaff and spindle for the benefit of her household and the poor — wisdom expressed in productive skill given to worthy ends. The leaders' bringing of precious stones points to the principle that those with more are expected to give more proportionally: "From everyone who has been given much, much will be required" (Luke 12:48).
4.christology: The people's voluntary generosity — bringing far more than was needed — points to the overflow of divine grace in Christ. Paul describes the Macedonian churches giving "beyond their means, of their own accord" (2 Cor 8:3), and grounds this generosity in "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor" (2 Cor 8:9). The tabernacle offerings were the first fruits of this Christological pattern: the generous One who gives himself generates a generous people who give themselves. The women who spun the finest thread for the holy place — their skilled hands consecrated to God's dwelling — are the first examples of what the NT calls every-believer priestly service: "present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God" (Rom 12:1).

30.synthesis:
Having received the offerings, Moses announces to the people what God had already told him on the mountain: the master craftsmen are divinely appointed. Bezalel son of Uri son of Hur, from Judah, is named first — "the Lord has called him by name" (Exod 35:30). His Spirit-filling is described in full: "with the Spirit of God, with skill, with intelligence, with knowledge, and with all craftsmanship, to devise artistic designs, to work in gold, silver, and bronze, in cutting stones for setting, and in carving wood, to work in every craft" (Exod 35:31-33). And God has given both Bezalel and Oholiab "ability to teach" — so that the Spirit-gift was not merely personal but pedagogical, reproducible, multiplied through the community of craftsmen.

Matthew Henry notes the remarkable phrase "called by name": "Those whom God calls by name to any service he will be sure to qualify for it." The divine calling and divine equipping are inseparable — God never assigns what he does not enable. The naming of Bezalel and Oholiab before the congregation served another purpose: it prevented strife about who should lead the work. With God having publicly designated the master craftsmen, the question of authority was settled before the first tool was lifted.

The addition of teaching ability (v 34) is significant for ecclesiology: the gifted person who cannot pass on their gifts keeps the community dependent on one person. God equips Bezalel and Oholiab not only to do the work but to instruct others in it, so that the entire community of craftsmen can participate. This multiplication of skill through teaching is the pattern of all healthy community: "what you have heard from me in the presence of many witnesses entrust to faithful men, who will be able to teach others also" (2 Tim 2:2).
30.historical_context: The repetition of Bezalel and Oholiab's appointment here (already given in ch 31) before the building begins serves the narrative purpose of public announcement. Chapter 31 was Moses receiving the information privately on the mountain; chapter 35 is Moses broadcasting it to the congregation. The distinction matters: the craftsmen work under public appointment, with accountability to Moses and to the congregation that brought the materials. The ability to teach (v 34) — unique to this second mention — suggests that the execution phase required not just two gifted individuals but a whole school of artisans whom Bezalel and Oholiab had to train on the job.
30.literary_structure: The Bezalel-Oholiab passage (vv 30-35) closes chapter 35, bridging the offering narrative (vv 4-29) with the construction narrative that begins in ch 36. The sequence is natural: materials gathered → craftsmen identified → construction begins. The Spirit-gifting described here is the most elaborate in the passage, including skill, intelligence, knowledge, craftsmanship, artistic design, and teaching — a comprehensive portrait of what God-equipped excellence looks like in practical work.
30.theological_application: The Spirit's equipping of craftsmen for sacred work establishes the principle that there is no domain of human skill that falls outside the Spirit's sanctifying reach. Matthew Henry applies this directly: "Skill in secular employments is God's gift, and comes from above." The instrumentalist who plays for worship, the architect who designs a church building, the programmer who builds tools for Christian community — all exercise God-given abilities in service of divine purposes. The same Spirit who gifted Bezalel and Oholiab gifts those who serve God's purposes in every generation, though "the same Spirit... allots to each one individually as he wills" (1 Cor 12:11).
30.christology: Bezalel's Spirit-filling to build the earthly tabernacle points to Christ, the Spirit-filled One who builds the heavenly reality: "Christ is faithful over God's house as a son" (Heb 3:6). As Bezalel's skill was the Spirit's gift and his design was the divine pattern shown to Moses, so Christ builds his church according to the Father's plan and by the Spirit's power. The teaching ability given to Bezalel anticipates Christ as the teacher who trained twelve to reproduce his ministry and send others: "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations" (Matt 28:19). Sacred skill is always reproductive, always looking to the next generation of workers who will continue the work when the master craftsman is no longer present.
